spec_reconstruction.domain: Current U.S. federal banking board obligations for Category I global systemically important bank holding companies and their insured depository institution subsidiaries.
spec_reconstruction.unit_of_analysis: Each discrete current, in-force board-level obligation, with precise source, duty, actor allocation, committee or director-type distinction, and reform relevance.
structural_axes: entity tier: Category I GSIB BHC versus insured depository institution subsidiary
actor: full board versus board committee
committee type: audit, risk, compensation, nominating/governance, fiduciary, compliance, technology, or other designated committee
director type: inside, outside, independent, non-independent, risk-expert, interested director, and disqualified director distinctions
source tier: statute, regulation, enforceable guideline, supervisory guidance, examination manual, enforcement template, and interagency standard
issuing authority: Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, CFPB, FFIEC/interagency, FinCEN/OFAC where relevant

regulatory domain: safety and soundness, capital, stress testing, liquidity, recovery, resolution, operational resilience, cybersecurity, third-party risk, model risk, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, incentive compensation, audit, internal controls, fiduciary, affiliate, and insider-transaction governance
actor_classes_required: Category I GSIB BHC full board
insured depository institution subsidiary full board
source_universe: Federal Reserve regulations, SR/CA letters, guidance, and examination/supervisory materials
OCC regulations, enforceable guidelines, bulletins, Comptroller's Handbook material, and licensing guidance
FDIC regulations, guidance, examination material, and IDI resolution-planning rules
CFPB supervision and examination materials relevant to consumer compliance governance
Federal statutes underlying board-level banking obligations
coverage_areas: consumer compliance, fair lending, UDAAP, privacy, and CRA oversight
audit, internal control, financial reporting, fiduciary, insider, affiliate, and director-qualification obligations
spec_reconstruction.audience_use: Bank directors, legal counsel, and policymakers seeking a working reform document to identify duplicative, overlapping, and burdensome board-level requirements.
spec_reconstruction.form_depth_expectations: Granular, citation-specific 40-60 page inventory with current-law status, actor mapping, committee/director distinctions, and reform-useful sludge analysis.

material_omissions: The report is a relatively compact 82-row inventory, not the requested 40-60 page citation-level working policy reform document.
CFPB consumer-compliance oversight is materially absent despite being an explicit source and domain in the original request.
Model risk governance is materially absent despite being an explicit required domain.
The inventory does not consistently separate Category I BHC duties from IDI subsidiary duties; bank-level and holding-company-level obligations are often mixed.
Resolution planning is treated mainly at the BHC level; FDIC CIDI resolution-planning obligations are not inventoried.
Several entries are not true board-level obligations but firm, management, CEO/CFO, or institution-level obligations recast as board duties.
Inside/outside and independent/non-independent director distinctions are covered for audit/risk committees but not systematically across the inventory.
Many citations are broad or secondary-source linked, with limited pinpoint primary-source support.

results.citation: Federal Reserve GSIB page for eight U.S. GSIBs
results.reason: The Federal Reserve lists the eight named U.S. GSIBs, including BNY Mellon and State Street: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/global-systemically-important-banks.htm

results.citation: SR 21-3 board effectiveness guidance
results.reason: SR 21-3 supports the five board-effectiveness attributes and board/management distinction: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/SR2103.htm

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.33(a)(1)-(a)(4) risk committee requirements
results.reason: Section 252.33 supports risk committee policy, charter, reporting, meeting, CRO report, and independent-chair requirements: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/252.33

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.33(b)(1) - board must appoint CRO
results.reason: The rule says the bank holding company must appoint a CRO; it does not state that the board itself must appoint the CRO.

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.34(a)-(b) liquidity risk tolerance and contingency funding plan
results.reason: Section 252.34 supports board approval of liquidity risk tolerance and risk committee approval of the contingency funding plan: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/252.34

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.54 and 12 CFR 252.44 as board stress-testing obligations
results.reason: Section 252.54 is a company-run stress-test rule and 252.44 is Board analysis; board consideration is better tied to 252.47, not the cited duties: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/252.47

results.citation: 12 CFR 252.62-.64 and 252.70-.78 as board compliance obligations
results.reason: TLAC, corporate-practices, and SCCL provisions impose firm requirements, but the sampled text does not impose discrete board duties: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/252.63

results.citation: 12 CFR 243.3(e) resolution-plan board approval
results.reason: The board approval duty is supported, but the current citation is 12 CFR 243.4(f), not 243.3(e): https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/243.4

results.citation: 12 U.S.C. 5641 / Dodd-Frank Section 956 as current mandatory board incentive-compensation duties
results.reason: Section 956 directs agencies to issue rules; GAO reported no final rule as of January 2025, and sampled sources show proposals rather than current final duties: https://files.gao.gov/reports/GAO-25-107032/index.html

results.citation: 12 CFR 248 Volcker compliance program and CEO attestation
results.reason: Section 248.20 supports the compliance-program and CEO attestation framework, though the report should separate CEO attestation from board obligation: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/248.20

results.citation: SR 12-17 and SR 14-8 recovery/corporate governance expectations
results.reason: SR 12-17 remains available and addresses corporate governance and recovery planning integration; SR 14-8 supports recovery-planning governance for certain large BHCs: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1217.htm

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix D OCC Heightened Standards
results.reason: Appendix D supports the risk-governance framework, risk appetite, CRE/CAE approval, independent director, training, and audit-function rows: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-I/part-30/appendix-Appendix%20D%20to%20Part%2030

results.citation: 17 CFR 229.106 / Regulation S-K Item 106 cybersecurity governance
results.reason: Item 106 requires disclosure of board oversight of cybersecurity risks, but it is a disclosure rule, not a prudential board-oversight mandate: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/17/229.106

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 363 audit committee requirements
results.reason: Part 363 supports audit committee outside-director, independence, expertise, outside counsel, and independent public accountant oversight requirements: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/12/part-363

results.citation: 31 CFR 1020.210 board approval of AML program
results.reason: Section 1020.210 requires a bank AML program approved by the board or equivalent governing body: https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/31/1020.210

results.citation: 31 CFR 1010.210(a)(2)/(a)(4)/(a)(5) as direct board BSA officer, training, and testing duties
results.reason: BSA program rules require responsible individual(s), training, and independent testing, but do not make each a direct board duty as stated.

results.citation: FFIEC Cybersecurity Assessment Tool obligations
results.reason: The CAT was voluntary and sunset on August 31, 2025; listing it as current 2026 guidance obligations is stale: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr2407.htm

results.citation: 2023 Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships
results.reason: The guidance supports board or designated committee oversight, risk-tolerance direction, and possible board approval for higher-risk/critical-activity plans: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/interagency-guidance-on-third-party-relationships.htm

results.citation: 12 CFR 215.4(a) and 12 U.S.C. 375b as board duties for insider lending terms and limits
results.reason: Regulation O does contain prior board approval for certain insider loans under 215.4(b), but the cited rows recast bank prohibitions as board ensure duties: https://ecfr.io/Title-12/Section-215.4

results.citation: 12 CFR 223 and 12 U.S.C. 371c as board duties for affiliate transactions
results.reason: Regulation W imposes member-bank transaction limits and restrictions, but the cited provisions do not impose the stated board-level duties: https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/topics/regulation_w.htm

actor_mapping.note: The report maps many duties to risk committee, audit committee, full board, CRO/CRE, and independent directors, but it does not consistently map BHC versus IDI subsidiary, compensation/nominating committees, or inside/outside director classes.

binding_vs_guidance.note: The Mandatory/Guidance type column is useful, but several entries labeled mandatory are actually firm, CEO/CFO, proposed, disclosure, or guidance expectations rather than direct board obligations.

reform_usefulness.note: The report provides concrete sludge zones and reform levers, including harmonization, safe harbors, committee equivalence, and consolidation of planning and reporting cycles.

critical_flags.evidence: The report cites "12 CFR 243.3(e)" for current resolution-plan board approval.
The report cites "12 CFR 252.54" for board stress-testing oversight.
The report lists "FFIEC Cybersecurity Assessment Tool" obligations despite noting it was sunsetted.
The report says "Board must implement policies for forfeiture and clawback" under Section 956.
The report says "Board must ensure compliance with credit exposure limits" under SCCL.
The report claims "The Complete Inventory: 82 Federal Board Obligations".
The report claims "82 distinct federal-level obligations" and "460+ board hours annually".

D1_coverage.evidence: The report claims "82 distinct federal-level obligations".
D1_coverage.rationale: The report has meaningful coverage of Fed, OCC, FDICIA, BSA, SEC, cyber, third-party, insider, and affiliate areas, but omits CFPB/consumer compliance and model risk and is not a full 40-60 page current inventory.

D2_integrity.evidence: The report cites "12 CFR 252.62-252.63" as board TLAC duties.
D2_integrity.rationale: Many citations are real and some support the rows, but central entries misattribute firm or CEO/CFO obligations to boards, use secondary sources where primary sources were needed, and include stale cyber guidance. F2/F7 cap the effective score.

D3_accuracy.evidence: The report labels Section 956 duties as "Mandatory".
D3_accuracy.rationale: The report is directionally sophisticated, but central current-law claims are wrong or overstated, especially Section 956, SCCL/TLAC/Reg W board duties, BSA officer designation, and sunsetted FFIEC CAT obligations. Central F3/F4 caps D3 at zero.

D4_sludge_analysis.evidence: The report identifies "The Ten Most Significant Sludge Zones".
D4_sludge_analysis.rationale: The sludge analysis is the strongest part: it identifies overlap clusters, estimates burden, and offers concrete agency-specific reform levers such as harmonization, safe harbors, and consolidated planning.

D5_structure.rationale: The structure is readable and more inventory-like than the other short reports, with numbered obligations and visual summaries. It still lacks exact actor/entity/director-type columns and is far shorter than requested.

D6_calibration.rationale: The mandatory/guidance distinction and legal-advice caveat help, but the report overclaims completeness, currentness, and quantified burden while several mandatory classifications are unsupported. F5 caps the effective score.

strengths: D5: The report is structured as a numbered inventory with citation and type columns, making it more usable than a prose-only memo.
D1: It correctly identifies the eight U.S. GSIBs and provides substantial coverage of Regulation YY, OCC Heightened Standards, FDICIA Part 363, BSA/AML, and third-party risk.

D4: The ten sludge zones are reform-actionable, with concrete recommendations for harmonizing risk committee, risk appetite, audit, ICFR, resilience, compensation, cyber, and third-party risk requirements.
D6: The report attempts to distinguish mandatory obligations from guidance-based expectations, which is important for directors and counsel.
weaknesses: D3: Several entries turn firm-level or management/CEO duties into board obligations, especially TLAC, SCCL, Regulation W, Regulation O, Section 956, and stress-testing data submissions.
D2: Important citations are wrong or stale, including current resolution-plan citation, stress-testing board duties, and FFIEC CAT obligations after the tool was sunsetted.
D1: CFPB consumer compliance and model risk governance are materially missing even though both were explicitly requested.
D5: The inventory does not consistently show whether the duty belongs to the BHC board, IDI board, full board, a specific committee, or a director type.
D6: The report overclaims with 'Complete Inventory,' 82 obligations, 71% mandatory, and 460+ hours without enough support for those conclusions.
prioritized_remediation: Re-verify all 82 rows against primary sources and relabel firm, management, CEO/CFO, proposed, guidance, and disclosure obligations separately from direct board duties.
Add missing CFPB consumer compliance and model risk sections with current primary guidance, including SR 26-2 for model risk.
Correct key citations: use 12 CFR 243.4(f) for BHC resolution-plan board approval, 12 CFR 252.47 for supervisory stress-test result consideration, and primary sources for SEC/SOX and FFIEC materials.
Add columns for entity tier, actor, committee, director type, frequency, documentation, binding status, and reform lever for every row.
Remove or qualify unsupported quantified burden estimates unless backed by a transparent methodology and source evidence.
narrative.reliance_recommendation: Use this as a strong sludge-analysis draft and obligation checklist, but not as a legal inventory until every row is primary-source verified and remapped by actor and entity tier.
